event: Sahelanthropus tchadensis, one of the oldest known hominins nicknamed Toumai, lived in the region of present-day northern Chad.
During the African Humid Period a 'Green Sahara' supported lakes, savanna, and early human communities across what is now Chad.
Progressive desiccation of the Sahara pushed populations southward toward the shrinking Lake Chad basin.
Settled farming and herding communities developed around Lake Chad, laying the basis for later state formation.
Trans-Saharan trade routes flourished, linking the Lake Chad region to North Africa through caravans carrying salt, gold, and slaves.
The Kanem Empire emerged northeast of Lake Chad, founded by the Sayfawa (Sefuwa) dynasty.
Mai Hummay (Hu) initiated a long-lasting Sayfawa dynasty and Kanem's rulers increasingly adopted Islam.
Under Mai Dunama Dabbalemi, the Kanem Empire reached its territorial height, controlling vast trans-Saharan trade networks.
Pressured by the Bulala people, the Sayfawa dynasty abandoned Kanem and relocated west of Lake Chad to Bornu.
The reconstituted Kanem-Bornu Empire consolidated power in Bornu, becoming a dominant Sahelian state.
Mai Idris Alooma began a celebrated reign, modernizing Kanem-Bornu's military with firearms and expanding its influence.
The kingdoms of Baguirmi and Ouaddai rose southeast of Lake Chad, becoming rivals and trade partners of Kanem-Bornu.
The Ouaddai Sultanate was established under Abd al-Karim, who overthrew the Tunjur rulers and promoted Islam.
Baguirmi, Ouaddai, and Bornu engaged in chronic warfare and raiding, with slave raids against southern non-Muslim peoples fueling the regional economy.
The Sudanese warlord Rabih az-Zubayr conquered Bornu and much of the Chad region, building a short-lived militarized empire.
French colonial forces defeated and killed Rabih az-Zubayr at the Battle of Kousseri, opening the region to French conquest.
Chad was incorporated into French Equatorial Africa as part of the Ubangi-Shari-Chad colony.
Chad became a separate French colony, though effective administration of the desert north remained limited.
Governor Felix Eboue made Chad the first French colony to rally to Free France under Charles de Gaulle during World War II.
Chad voted to become an autonomous republic within the French Community.
Chad gained full independence from France on 11 August, with Francois Tombalbaye becoming its first president.
Chad was admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
Tombalbaye banned opposition parties and established a one-party state under the PPT.
Tax revolts and northern grievances sparked rebellion, marking the start of decades of civil war.
The northern rebel movement FROLINAT (National Liberation Front of Chad) was founded, opposing the southern-dominated government.
President Tombalbaye was assassinated in a military coup led by Felix Malloum.
Government collapse plunged Chad into full-scale civil war among rival factions, with Goukouni Oueddei emerging as president.
Libya intervened militarily on behalf of Goukouni, deepening foreign involvement in Chad's conflict.
Hissene Habre seized power in N'Djamena, beginning an authoritarian rule marked by widespread repression.
Chadian forces decisively defeated Libyan troops in the 'Toyota War,' largely expelling Libya from the disputed Aozou Strip.
Idriss Deby overthrew Habre and seized power, ruling Chad for the next three decades.
The International Court of Justice ruled in Chad's favor over the Aozou Strip, confirming sovereignty over the border region with Libya.
Chad adopted a new constitution and Idriss Deby won the country's first multiparty presidential election.
The Chad-Cameroon Pipeline began exporting oil, transforming the national economy and raising revenue-management controversies.
Conflict spilled over from Sudan's Darfur region as rebels and refugees crossed into eastern Chad.
Rebel forces attacked N'Djamena but were repelled, as Deby won a disputed third presidential term.
Rebel columns again reached the capital N'Djamena before being driven back by government forces.
Chad and Sudan signed an accord to normalize relations and stop supporting each other's rebel movements.
Chadian troops played a leading role in the French-led intervention against jihadists in northern Mali.
Chad joined a regional coalition against Boko Haram, which carried out deadly suicide bombings in N'Djamena.
Hissene Habre was convicted of crimes against humanity by a special court in Senegal, a landmark in international justice.
President Idriss Deby was killed during fighting against rebels; his son Mahamat Deby took power as head of a military transitional council.
Security forces violently suppressed opposition protests against the prolonged military transition, killing dozens in N'Djamena.
Mahamat Deby won a contested presidential election, formalizing his rule and ending the military transition period.
